headline: Sustained external visual lock with a t=22s shift to dorsal attention; falsification flat (delta 0.0, generic_plausible).
synthesis_paragraph: The clip held the viewer in sustained external focus. Visual processing maintained a moderate hum across the first twenty-two seconds, parsing surfaces and geometry without face or social signals; the default-mode specialist receded almost entirely, consistent with attention locked outward rather than inward. Around t=22s the dominant signature shifted from visual to dorsal attention — the attention specialist's reading aligns with a transition from passive viewing toward more goal-directed scanning, though the magnitudes stayed modest. Somatomotor and limbic circuits remained near baseline throughout: no embodied movement simulation, no affective tagging. At t=30s the ventral attention specialist registered its single peak of the clip, marking a discrete event boundary against an otherwise monotonous stimulus. The arc is one of low-load external observation, capped by a brief salience flicker — no sustained deliberation, no emotional coloring, no inner narrative engaged.

temporal_arc.scene: Vision report is unavailable for this clip; the opening segment shows continuous external observation with no annotated events.
temporal_arc.claim: The visual specialist's reading describes sustained processing of a static or slowly changing scene, and the activation profile matches: a steady ramp without sharp transients, consistent with the eye settling onto a stable external field.

temporal_arc.scene: Middle segment of continuous engagement per the vision stub; no discrete events annotated.
temporal_arc.brain_signature: Visual network sustained at peak (0.30-0.33); dorsal attention idling beneath it.
temporal_arc.claim: The dorsal-attention specialist read this as moderate sustained attention without effortful search. The flat plateau in visual activation, paired with subdued frontoparietal, is consistent with passive viewing rather than active tracking.

temporal_arc.scene: Late-middle segment; vision report shows no transition events.
temporal_arc.brain_signature: Visual decaying (0.27 → 0.13); dorsal attention beginning to climb.
temporal_arc.claim: The visual signal sheds amplitude while dorsal attention rises — a handoff the swarm specialists characterized as light, intermittent attentional engagement rather than a deliberate task switch.

temporal_arc.scene: Concluding segment of the clip per the vision stub.
temporal_arc.brain_signature: Dorsal attention takes the top slot (peak 0.20 at t=26s).
temporal_arc.claim: The dorsal-attention specialist's reading of brief attentional capture maps onto this window's dominance shift. Magnitudes remain modest, so this reads as orienting rather than effortful search.

temporal_arc.brain_signature: Ventral attention emerges as top network (0.14) — its only peak of the clip.
temporal_arc.claim: The ventral-attention specialist flagged this as a discrete event boundary against an otherwise predictable stimulus. The terminal salience flicker is the clip's clearest punctuation mark.

neural_evidence.what_it_processed: Sustained processing of a static or slowly changing scene with moderate engagement and no face/social signals.
neural_evidence.contribution: Visual dominance for the first twenty-two seconds is the load-bearing signal of the clip. The paragraph's external-lock framing rests on this region's sustained plateau.

neural_evidence.what_it_processed: Near-silent across the clip; mean essentially at baseline with a small terminal peak (0.106) at t=30s.
neural_evidence.contribution: Its silence corroborates the absence of embodied simulation or action mirroring. The terminal blip contributes to the multimodal punctuation at clip's end without implying movement content.

neural_evidence.what_it_processed: Moderate sustained attention with intermittent engagement; ascends to top-network status from t=22s onward.
neural_evidence.contribution: This region's late-clip dominance is what differentiates the second half from the first; the paragraph's handoff narrative is grounded in its trajectory.

neural_evidence.what_it_processed: Extremely low baseline with a single modest peak at t=30s, consistent with one discrete unexpected event.
neural_evidence.contribution: This is the clip's only salience signal, and it carries the inflection moment. Without it, the clip would read as flat throughout.

neural_evidence.what_it_processed: Emotionally neutral throughout — no reward, threat, or personal-meaning tagging detected.
neural_evidence.contribution: Sustained limbic silence rules out an affective interpretation of the clip and supports the low-load characterization.

neural_evidence.what_it_processed: Minimal cognitive control demand throughout, with a brief task-relevant flicker at t=30s.
neural_evidence.contribution: Its quietness underwrites the 'no sustained deliberation' verdict. The terminal flicker contributes to the multimodal salience event but does not indicate extended working-memory load.

neural_evidence.what_it_processed: Suppressed throughout, anti-correlated with visual and dorsal attention dominance.
neural_evidence.contribution: Default-mode suppression is the structural counterpart to the external-lock reading. It is the clearest single piece of evidence that attention was not turned inward.

inflection_moment.scene_event: Vision report is unavailable; the activity table shows the dominant network transitioning at this second.
inflection_moment.neural_event: The top-region signature shifts from visual (which had held for 22 consecutive seconds) to dorsal attention, with visual amplitude decaying and dorsal attention beginning a sustained climb.
inflection_moment.why_it_matters: This is the only structural pivot in the clip — the handoff from sustained passive viewing to light goal-directed scanning. It is the boundary the swarm's dorsal-attention specialist implicitly tracked when it described intermittent engagement.

falsification.explanation: Main and control paragraph scores are identical, yielding zero delta. The paragraph's load-bearing claims — sustained visual dominance, default-mode suppression, limbic silence — are common across many low-arousal observational clips, so even strong per-region anchoring (limbic 1.0, ventral attention 0.95, default-mode 0.95) does not differentiate this clip's brain pattern from the control's. The result is honest: the description fits, but it could fit elsewhere too.

ironside.risk_flag: t=30s — coordinated somatomotor, ventral attention, and frontoparietal flicker against an otherwise flat baseline; salience specialist registered a discrete event the rest of the clip did not prepare the worker for.
ironside.training_target: Dorsal attention stayed in the moderate-passive range across the full clip; deliberate-scanning practice would build sustained goal-directed attention rather than the intermittent orienting observed here.
